caption: Figure 2: The general fake data generation pipeline of the proposed DF40 dataset.

Figure 6: Logits and confidence distribution analysis. We visualize the logits distribution (before SoftMax) and confidence distribution (after SoftMax) for both real and fake classes. The overlap in the logits distribution represents the portion of "wrong classifications." We analyze two models: Xception [67] and SBI [70]. More discussion and analysis can be referred to Sec. 5.

Figure 7: t-SNE Visualizations for three models: CLIP-large, CLIP-base, and Xception (from left to right). These models are trained on FS (FF). We conduct visualizations on FS (CDF), HeyGen [28], MidJourney [53], and WhichisReal [53]. More discussion and analysis can be referred to Sec. 5.

Table 16: Part of quantitative results of OvA (Protocol-4): Testing on EFS (CDF) and Training on DF40 (FF). EFS (FF) denotes all EFS data within the FF domain, and DF40 (FF) denotes the combination results of FS (CDF), FR (CDF), and EFS (CDF).
Figure 15: Illustration of visual examples for all unknown forgery data, including all types like FS, FR, EFS, and FE, simulating the real-world SoTA deepfakes.

Figure 1: Overview of our DF40 dataset. DF40 shows advantages in data diversity, synthesis quality, and deepfake realism. Note all the above figures are deepfake, which does not exist in the real world.

Table 4: Same forgery types, different data domains (Protocol-2): Cross-domain evaluation of different models on CDF domain. Within-forgery evaluations are in gray. FS (FF) denotes all FS data within the FF domain, with FR (FF), FS (CDF), etc, having similar meanings.

Table 3: Same data domain, different forgery types (Protocol-1): Cross-manipulation evaluation of different models on FF domain. Within-forgery evaluations are in gray. FS (FF) denotes all FS data within FF domain, with FR (FF), FS (CDF), etc, being similar.
Figure 14: Illustration of visual examples for all EFS (entire face synthesis) generated data on the FF domain.
Table 7: Similar to Tab. 6, we evaluate on GenImage [102] (non-face domain), pure AIGCs.
Table 6: Comparison of different models/baselines used for pre-training. FS (CDF) denotes all FS data within the CDF domain. DF40 (FF) is the combination of FS (FF), FR (FF), and EFS (FF).

Table 9: Results of evaluating three models (i.e., I3D, CLIP-base, and CLIP-large) trained on different variants of the proposed DF40 on other previous deepfake datasets. Note that only FF-F2F [77] and FF-NT [76] are FR methods, while other testing data all belong to FS.

Table 10: Summary of the datasets used for deepfake detection. The table provides information on the number of real and fake videos, the total number of videos, whether rights have been cleared, the number of agreeing subjects, the total number of subjects, the number of synthesis methods, and the number of perturbations.

Table 1: Comparison of existing/previous deepfake datasets. DF40 surpasses any other dataset in diversity, scale, and modeling. Deepfake data in DF40 is created by 40 deepfake techniques, including 10 FS methods, 13 FR methods, 12 EFS methods, and 5 FE methods.

Table 15: Part of quantitative results of OvA (Protocol-4): Testing on FR (CDF) and Training on DF40 (FF). FR (FF) denotes all FR data within the FF domain, and DF40 (FF) denotes the combination results of FS (CDF), FR (CDF), and EFS (CDF).

Table 14: Part of quantitative results of OvA (Protocol-4): Testing on FS (CDF) and Training on DF40 (FF). FS (FF) denotes all FS data within the FF domain, and DF40 (FF) denotes the combination results of FS (CDF), FR (CDF), and EFS (CDF).

Table 13: Part of quantitative results of OvA (Protocol-4): Testing on EFS (FF) and Training on DF40 (FF). EFS (FF) denotes all EFS data within the FF domain, and DF40 (FF) denotes the combination results of FS (FF), FR (FF), and EFS (FF).

Table 12: Part of quantitative results of OvA (Protocol-4): Testing on FR (FF) and Training on DF40 (FF). FR (FF) denotes all FR data within the FF domain, and DF40 (FF) denotes the combination results of FS (FF), FR (FF), and EFS (FF).
Figure 13: Illustration of visual examples for all FR (face-reenactment) generated data on the FF domain.
Table 8: Evaluation of training CLIP-large on different variants of the proposed DF40 (face domain) to detect GenImage [102] (non-face domain), pure AIGCs.

Figure 8: Frequency analysis on selected datasets of DF40. We compute the average frequency spectra of high-pass filtered images, following [82]. Although the deepfake types differ, they can show similar patterns/artifacts in the average spectra. For instance, methods from FS (i.e., SimSwap [13], BlendFace [71], InSwap) show similar "checkboard" patterns that can also be observed in EFS (i.e., SD1.5 [64], VQGAN [24]) and FE (i.e., e4e [78]).

Figure 9: Results of evaluating three (previous) SoTA models (trained on FF++), i.e., Xception [67], SPSL [48], and SBI [70]), on our DF40 (CDF). We show that, in most cases, these SoTAs fail to generalize well in our proposed forgeries, highlighting the importance of developing a comprehensive and diverse dataset.

Figure 4: Full results of SBI [70] on the proposed DF40 dataset for both the FF (within) and CDF domain (cross). Discussion and analysis for the figure can be seen in Sec. 5.1. We evaluate other detectors, including Xception [67] and SPSL [48]) on our DF40 (CDF) in Fig. 9.

Table 5: Different forgery types, different data domains (Protocol-3): toward real-world open-set evaluation of different models on the unknown domain. FS (FF) denotes all FS data within the FF domain, with FR (FF) and EFS (FF) being similar. BI donates using "BlendFake" only to train. Here, we use ❶ to mark the FS method, ❷ for the FR, ❸ for the EFS, and ❹ for the FE.

Figure 3: Heatmaps of One-Verse-All (OvA) evaluation (Protocol-4): Training the baseline (i.e., Xception) on one fake and testing it on other remaining fakes. We show the within-domain evaluation (FF++ domain) and cross-domain evaluation (CDF domain). We also show the performance "drop" from the FF++ to the CDF. Blue donates all FS methods, Green for FR, and Yellow for EFS.
Figure 17: Std of the frequency analysis for visualizing different deepfake methods from the frequency domain.
